coredata.dc:title: Superbright organosilica nanodots as a universal sensor for fast discrimination and accurate quantification of live/dead cells
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Xiaokai
Zhang, Xiaodong
Li, Chengcheng
Sayed, Sayed Mir
Sun, Wei
Lin, Fengming
Wu, Fu-Gen
coredata.dc:description: Rapid and quantitative discrimination between live and dead cells is highly desirable yet remains a challenging task. Here, we report the use of the ultrabright green-emitting organosilica nanodots (OSiNDs, photoluminescence quantum yield: ˜100%) for fast (1 min in vitro and 5 min in vivo), wash-free, and universal labeling of the dead bacterial, fungal, and mammalian cells, which meets the demand of the green fluorescent probes that can specifically image the dead cells. The photostability of OSiNDs surpasses that of the commercial dyes, such as propidium iodide (PI) and SYTOX Green nucleic acid stain (SYTOX), making OSiNDs more suitable for long-term and stable monitoring of the cell viability. With the assistance of a commercial red fluorescent dye SYTO 60, we develop an in situ method for rapid and accurate quantification of live/dead cells. Motivated by the unique optical advantages and high sensitivity of OSiNDs to the cell viability, the OSiNDs are employed to observe the in vivo cellular changes induced by doxorubicin using zebrafish as a model, demonstrating their great potential for evaluating the toxicity/efficacy of the therapeutic agents in real time. Owing to the excellent biocompatibility and low cost, the OSiNDs can be a universal probe for efficient and accurate differentiation and quantification of the live and dead cells, which may find wide applications in the biomedical field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silicon-containing nanomaterials
Live/dead discrimination
Single cell analysis
Drug efficiency evaluation
Fluorescence imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Staining performance of OSiNDs in live and dead S. aureus cells without washing
Uptake mechanisms of OSiNDs by live microbial and mammalian cells
Interaction of OSiNDs with deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) or ribonucleic acid (RNA)
Photostability comparison of OSiNDs, SYTOX, and PI
Quantification of live/dead cells using SYTO 60 and OSiNDs
Universal discrimination between live and dead cells in vitro and in vivo

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Confocal fluorescence images of live and dead S. aureus cells treated with various concentrations of OSiNDs, and (b) corresponding flow cytometric results. (c) Confocal fluorescence images of live or dead S. aureus cells treated with OSiNDs (20 μg/mL) for different time periods. (d) Flow cytometric results of dead S. aureus cells treated with OSiNDs (20 μg/mL) for different time periods. (e) Flow cytometric results of live S. aureus cells treated with OSiNDs (20 μg/mL) for different time periods. (f) Effect of 4 °C/NaN3 treatment on the fluorescence intensity of OSiND (20 μg/mL)-treated S. aureus cells as measured by flow cytometry. (g) Colocalization fluorescence images of OSiNDs (20 μg/mL) and PI (30 μM) in dead S. aureus cells. (h) Dynamic light scattering (DLS) results of DNA, DNA + OSiNDs, RNA, and RNA + OSiNDs. (I) Schematic illustration of the live/dead cell discrimination mechanism of OSiNDs. (j) Photostability evaluation results of SYTOX, OSiNDs, and PI in S. aureus cells via confocal fluorescence imaging. Scale bar =10 μm.

(a) Confocal fluorescence images and (b) the corresponding flow cytometric results of bacterial suspensions prepared by mixing the dead S. aureus cells and live ones at a number ratio of 5:5, 6:4, 7:3, 8:2, and 9:1, respectively. Before analysis, the bacterial suspensions were co-stained with SYTO 60 (50 nM) and OSiNDs (20 μg/mL). Scale bar =10 μm.

Confocal fluorescence images showing the discrimination between live/dead (a) bacteria (E. coli), (b) fungi (P. pastoris), (c) mammalian cells (A549), and (d) zebrafish using OSiNDs (20 μg/mL) as a universal imaging probe. Flow cytometric results of live/dead (e) bacteria (E. coli), (f) fungi (P. pastoris), and (g) mammalian cells (A549) after incubation with OSiNDs (20 μg/mL, 1 min). (h) Schematic illustrating the universal imaging capability of OSiNDs for dead bacterial, fungal, and mammalian cells. (i) Photographs of the agar plates and (j) the corresponding statistical results of the colonies formed by S. aureus treated with various concentrations of OSiNDs or PI for 24 h. (k) Cytotoxicity evaluation results of A549 cells after treatment with different concentrations of OSiNDs or PI. Scale bars in (a–c) =10 μm. Scale bar in (d) = 500 μm.

head.ce:title: Superbright organosilica nanodots as a universal sensor for fast discrimination and accurate quantification of live/dead cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Bioelectronics, School of Biological Science and Medical Engineering, Southeast University, 2 Sipailou Road, Nanjing, 210096, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Biological Science and Medical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biotechnology, Jiangnan University, Wuxi, 214122, Jiangsu, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rapid and quantitative discrimination between live and dead cells is highly desirable yet remains a challenging task. Here, we report the use of the ultrabright green-emitting organosilica nanodots (OSiNDs, photoluminescence quantum yield: ˜100%) for fast (1 min in vitro and 5 min in vivo), wash-free, and universal labeling of the dead bacterial, fungal, and mammalian cells, which meets the demand of the green fluorescent probes that can specifically image the dead cells. The photostability of OSiNDs surpasses that of the commercial dyes, such as propidium iodide (PI) and SYTOX Green nucleic acid stain (SYTOX), making OSiNDs more suitable for long-term and stable monitoring of the cell viability. With the assistance of a commercial red fluorescent dye SYTO 60, we develop an in situ method for rapid and accurate quantification of live/dead cells. Motivated by the unique optical advantages and high sensitivity of OSiNDs to the cell viability, the OSiNDs are employed to observe the in vivo cellular changes induced by doxorubicin using zebrafish as a model, demonstrating their great potential for evaluating the toxicity/efficacy of the therapeutic agents in real time. Owing to the excellent biocompatibility and low cost, the OSiNDs can be a universal probe for efficient and accurate differentiation and quantification of the live and dead cells, which may find wide applications in the biomedical field.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer and microbial infections cause millions of human death annually. Many efforts have been devoted to cancer therapy and treatment of microbial infections, during which the distinguishment between live and dead cells is of great importance [1–7]. However, an efficient and universal method for rapid differentiation and accurate quantification of live/dead cells is still lacking. Currently, several typical methods, such as electron microscopy [8], atomic force microscopy [9], Raman spectroscopy [10,11], and synchrotron infrared microspectrometry [12] have been developed to recognize live/dead microbial or mammalian cells. Nevertheless, most of the above methods have limitations, such as long operation time, complicated procedures, and requirement of cell fixation, which make them not suitable for real-time cell viability monitoring and rapid and direct quantification of the live/dead cells.

Compared with the above-mentioned methods, fluorescence labeling technique has become one of the most frequently used methods due to its advantages of simple operation, rapid responses, and high sensitivity [13–19]. Up to date, several fluorescent organic molecules and nanoparticles have been developed to differentiate live cells from dead ones, and they are classified into two categories (type I and II) according to their differentiation mechanisms. The materials in type I include propidium iodide (PI) [20], SYTOX Green nucleic acid stain (abbreviated as SYTOX) [21], and carbon dots [22–25], which can stain the dead cells via compromised membrane integrity, while the materials in type II such as fluorescein diacetate [26] and 4-methylumbelliferyl-β-d-glucuronide [27] can selectively stain live cells due to the difference between the metabolic capabilities of the dead and live cells. Nowadays, red-emitting PI and green-emitting SYTOX are used as commercial reagents for dead cell imaging, but the former has the drawback of high toxicity and the latter has the limitation of poor photostability. As a class of alternative materials to stain dead cells, carbon dots usually have multicolor fluorescence emission, which overlaps with that of the live cell probes and severely hinders their practical applications. Thus, it is highly desirable to develop new fluorescent materials with superior optical and biocompatible properties for achieving rapid and sensitive differentiation between live and dead cells.

Besides the distinguishment of live cells from dead ones, the quantification of the two types of cells is also important. Live/dead quantification agents that are commonly used nowadays include 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyl-2-H-tetrazolium bromide (MTT), trypan blue, Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) kit, Live/Dead Baclight bacterial viability kit using SYTO 9 and PI, Live/Dead cell viability kit using calcein-AM and PI, etc. The MTT assay needs complicated procedures and long operation time, and it is not suitable for microbial cell analyses. In addition, the MTT assay cannot be used for long-term cell viability monitoring and in vivo monitoring, because the live cells will be killed after treatment with the highly toxic MTT. For trypan blue assay, false positive results may be obtained due to the high toxicity of trypan blue, making it unsuitable for long-term analysis. In terms of CCK-8 assay, it may cause inconvenience because it needs to run pilot tests to determine the optimal incubation time. Moreover, the price of Live/Dead Backlight bacterial viability kit and Live/Dead cell viability assay kit is very high. Hence, it is necessary to develop an alternative method to meet the demand of universal and accurate live/dead cell quantification with both high efficiency and low cost.

Recently, fluorescent silicon-containing nanoparticles (SiNPs) have attracted substantial research interest owing to their wide range of applications in the biomedical field [28–39]. As an emerging class of fluorescent nanomaterials, SiNPs possess the optical advantages of excitation wavelength-independent emission to avoid fluorescence overlap, high photoluminescence quantum yields, favorable anti-photobleaching ability, and excellent biocompatibility [40–43]. In a recent study, we have reported the fabrication of ultrabright green-emitting organosilica nanodots (OSiNDs) with 100% photoluminescence quantum efficiency and have demonstrated that the highly photostable and biocompatible OSiNDs can be used as a long-term lysosomal tracker for living, fixed, and permeabilized mammalian cells [44]. Here, we report the application of the designed OSiNDs as a fluorescent probe to realize fast, efficient, and universal differentiation of live/dead mammalian and microbial cells. In addition, the feasibility of using the as-prepared OSiNDs to realize the in vivo drug toxicity/efficacy was also evaluated using the zebrafish model. We hope that the designed OSiNDs can be used as a low-cost and effective method for the precise quantification of live and dead cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Staining performance of OSiNDs in live and dead S. aureus cells without washing
ce:section.ce:para: Live and dead S. aureus cells (1 × 108 CFU/mL, 1 mL) were separately exposed to different concentrations of OSiNDs (10, 20, 50, or 100 μg/mL) for different time periods (0, 1, 3, 5, 10, 30, 60, or 120 min) and then co-stained with PI (30 μM) for 5 min. Then, the cells were analyzed by flow cytometry using a flow cytometer (NovoCyte 2060, ACEA) and imaged under a confocal microscope (TCS SP8, Leica, Germany). The OSiNDs were excited at 488 nm and the emission was collected in the range of 495–540 nm. PI was excited at 552 nm and the emission was collected in the range of 600–650 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Uptake mechanisms of OSiNDs by live microbial and mammalian cells
ce:section.ce:para: For comparative analysis, the S. aureus and A549 cells were chosen as representatives of the microbial and mammalian cells, respectively. Briefly, S. aureus cells (1 × 108 CFU/mL, 1 mL) were divided into three groups (group I, II, and III). The cells in group I were cultured in a shaking incubator (200 rpm) at 37 °C and the cells in group II were pretreated with NaN3 (final concentration: 3 mM) for 30 min, while those in group III were cultured at 4 °C for 30 min. Then OSiNDs with a final concentration of 20 μg/mL were added into the above three groups. After incubation for another 30 min, the intracellular fluorescence intensities of OSiNDs were analyzed using flow cytometry.

Similarly, A549 cells (1 × 104 cells/well in 96-well plates) were also divided into three groups (group I, II, and III). The cells in group I were cultured in a humidified incubator at 37 °C and 5% CO2 and the cells in group II were pretreated with NaN3 (final concentration: 10 mM) for 2 h, while those in group III were cultured at 4 °C for 2 h. Then OSiNDs with a final concentration of 20 μg/mL were added into the above three groups. After incubation for another 2 h, the intracellular fluorescence intensities of OSiNDs were analyzed using flow cytometry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction of OSiNDs with deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) or ribonucleic acid (RNA)
ce:section.ce:para: The interaction of OSiNDs with DNA and RNA was also studied. Briefly, the DNA or RNA molecules were extracted from S. aureus cells using an Ezup column bacteria genomic DNA purification kit (Sangon Biotech, China) and a spin column bacteria total RNA purification kit (Sangon Biotech, China), respectively. Then the hydrodynamic diameters and zeta potentials of OSiNDs, DNA, DNA + OSiNDs, RNA, and RNA + OSiNDs were measured by a zetasizer instrument (Nano ZS, Malvern).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photostability comparison of OSiNDs, SYTOX, and PI
ce:section.ce:para: A comparative analysis of the photostability of OSiNDs, SYTOX, and PI was also performed. Dead S. aureus cells (1 × 108 CFU/mL, 1 mL) were incubated with OSiNDs (final concentration: 20 μg/mL), SYTOX 60 (final concentration: 1 μM), or PI (final concentration: 30 μM) for 5 min. After irradiation under 488 or 552 nm (at 2% of its maximum output, objective: 100 × /1.40, zoom factor: 3.30) for different time periods (0, 0.5, 1, 3, 5, 10, 15, 20, or 30 min), the bacterial cells were imaged using the confocal microscope. The corresponding fluorescence intensities of the bacterial cells were analyzed using the ImageJ software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of live/dead cells using SYTO 60 and OSiNDs
ce:section.ce:para: A mixture of SYTO 60 (final concentration: 5 μM) and OSiNDs (final concentration: 20 μg/mL) was added to the bacterial suspensions (1 × 108 CFU/mL, 1 mL) containing different ratios of live/dead S. aureus cells for 5 min. Then the bacterial suspensions were centrifugated at 7000 rpm for 5 min. Next, the supernatant was removed and the bacteria were resuspended in normal saline. Finally, confocal microscopy and flow cytometry were used for observation and quantification of the live/dead cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Universal discrimination between live and dead cells in vitro and in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: For in vitro staining, the live/dead bacteria (1 × 108 CFU/mL, 1 mL), fungi (1 × 108 CFU/mL, 1 mL), and cancerous/normal mammalian cells (1 × 104 cells/well in 96-well plates) were incubated with OSiNDs (final concentration: 20 μg/mL) for 1 min. Then the cells were analyzed by flow cytometry and confocal microscopy without washing. For in vivo discrimination, live/dead zebrafish embryos or adults (one embryo/well or one adult/well in 96-well plates) were exposed to OSiNDs (100 μg/mL) for 5 min and observed under the confocal microscope without washing. The emission was detected in the range of 495–540 nm at an excitation wavelength of 488 nm.

The OSiNDs prepared according to the previous report [44] have an average diameter of ∼1.8 nm (Fig. S1a) and ultrabright green fluorescence under UV light irradiation with excitation and emission peaks observed at 511 and 525 nm, respectively (Fig. S1b). To begin with, we evaluated the imaging performance of OSiNDs for bacterial cells. Taking Gram-positive bacteria Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) as a representative, it was found that the live cells were hardly stained by all the tested concentrations of OSiNDs, while strong fluorescence signals were observed in dead S. aureus cells (obtained by treatment with 1% benzalkonium bromide for 2 h) even at an OSiND concentration as low as 10 μg/mL (Fig. 1 a). The flow cytometric results further show that the intracellular content of OSiNDs in dead bacteria was much higher than that in the live ones for all the tested concentrations (Fig. 1b). Next, we evaluated the effect of the staining time on the live/dead cell discrimination performance of OSiNDs. The dead S. aureus cells exhibited strong green fluorescence after incubation with OSiNDs for only 1 min (Fig. 1c), and the fluorescence intensity at 1 min was as high as that at 3, 5, and 10 min (Fig. 1d). Moreover, the culture medium had a negligible influence on the imaging performance, indicating the ultrafast and complete penetration of OSiNDs. The above results demonstrate the favorable wash-free and rapid staining ability of OSiNDs. On the other hand, very few OSiNDs entered the live S. aureus cells even when the incubation time increased to 2 h (Fig. 1e), indicating that the intact cell surfaces of the live bacteria were impermeable to the nanodots. To investigate whether energy supply can influence the internalization of OSiNDs, sodium azide (NaN3) and low temperature (4 °C) treatments were adopted to deplete the intracellular adenosine triphosphate (ATP) and decrease the activity of enzymes to lower the production of ATP, respectively [45,46]. Fig. 1f shows that the intracellular fluorescence intensity of OSiNDs was decreased to 68% and 72% after the treatment of 4 °C and NaN3, respectively, indicating that the internalization of OSiNDs in living cells may be energy-dependent.

To validate the selective dead cell imaging ability of OSiNDs, a commercial dead cell imaging probe PI was used. Strong yellow signals were observed in the merged confocal image (Fig. 1g), which indicates the excellent colocalization of green fluorescent OSiNDs and red fluorescent PI, and also confirms that OSiNDs can specifically image the dead cells. To investigate the staining mechanism, the DNA and RNA were extracted separately from the S. aureus cells and then incubated with OSiNDs. It was found that after addition of OSiNDs, the hydrodynamic diameters and zeta potentials of DNA + OSiNDs and RNA + OSiNDs were significantly changed compared with those of free OSiNDs (Fig. S2) and DNA/RNA (Figs. 1h and S3), indicating the strong interactions between amine-rich OSiNDs and negatively charged DNA/RNA. The live/dead cell differentiation mechanism of OSiNDs can be explained by the marked difference in the uptake pathways of the two types of cells, as shown in Fig. 1i. Compared to the slow internalization of OSiNDs in the live cells, the dead cells with damaged surfaces (Fig. S4) allowed the rapid penetration of OSiNDs by passive diffusion. The internalized OSiNDs strongly interacted with DNA and RNA, and thus only the dead cells were lit up, realizing the effective differentiation between live and dead cells.

In addition, considering the favorable anti-photobleaching property of OSiNDs described in the previous report [44], we compared the photostability of OSiNDs with that of other commercial reagents, including green fluorescent SYTOX and red fluorescent PI dyes, which are widely used dyes to label the dead cells. Contrary to the photobleaching effect of SYTOX (whose fluorescence intensity was quickly quenched within 30 s), OSiNDs exhibited strong fluorescence even after 30 min of irradiation, surpassing the performance of PI whose fluorescence significantly decreased within 15 min (Fig. 1j). The excellent photostability of OSiNDs was further confirmed by the corresponding fluorescence quantification results, as shown in Fig. S5. The above results indicate the potential of OSiNDs for long-term stable monitoring of the dead cells. Moreover, the OSiNDs will meet the demand of the green-emitting probes to label dead cells due to their outstanding optical properties, including high photoluminescence quantum yield of ∼100% and favorable anti-bleaching performance.

Encouraged by the above qualitative results, we further used OSiNDs to quantitatively distinguish live and dead cells with the assistance of the red fluorescent SYTO 60 stain, which is a cell-permeable nucleic acid stain that can label both live and dead cells. A series of suspensions containing various number ratios of live S. aureus cells to dead ones were prepared and exposed to SYTO 60 (5 μM) and OSiNDs (20 μg/mL) for 5 min. When observed under a confocal microscope, all cells were marked with red fluorescent SYTO 60, whereas only the dead cells were lit up by green fluorescent OSiNDs. From the merged images, we can easily differentiate live and dead cells; the live cells emit red fluorescence while the dead ones emit strong yellow fluorescence, as shown in Fig. 2 a. To further verify the reliability of such a fluorescence-based bacterial quantification assay, the bacterial suspensions were simultaneously analyzed by flow cytometry. As presented in Fig. 2b, two distinct regions (group I marked by a green circle and group II marked by a red circle) can be seen, which are attributed to the dead and live cells, respectively. The proportions of the cells stained with OSiNDs increased with the increase in the ratio of the dead cells. Particularly, the detected ratios of the dead to live cells are in good accordance with the expected values, confirming the high sensitivity and accuracy of this method.

The live/dead cell discrimination ability of OSiNDs was also analyzed in other types of cells. Besides the above Gram-positive bacteria S. aureus cells, other cells such as Gram-negative bacteria Escherichia coli (E. coli) cells, fungal cells, including Pichia. pastoris (P. pastoris) and Trichoderma reesei (T. reesei), cancerous mammalian cells, including A549 (human lung cancer cell line) and MC-38 (mouse colon adenocarcinoma cell line), and normal mammalian cells, including AT II (normal lung cell line) and MCF-10A (normal breast epithelial cell lines), were chosen for live/dead staining assay. The efficient (within 1 min) differentiation between live and dead cells was realized using OSiNDs (Figs. 3 a–c, e–g, and S6), indicating that OSiNDs can be a universal probe to differentiate live cells from dead ones regardless of the cell types. The differentiation mechanism of OSiNDs results from their distinct uptake pathways in live and dead cells: The OSiNDs are slowly internalized into live microbial/mammalian cells via an energy-dependent pathway (taking S. aureus and A549 cells as the representatives of microbial and mammalian cells, respectively, Figs. 1f and S7); however, they rapidly diffuse into dead cells with damaged surfaces (Fig. S4). On the other hand, to verify the live/dead differentiation performance of OSiNDs, we prepared various dead mammalian/microbial cells using different inactivation methods, such as benzalkonium bromide, vancomycin, ethanol, and Triton X-100 treatments. As expected, all the dead cells were labeled by green fluorescent OSiNDs (Fig. S8), indicating that the dead cell imaging performance of OSiNDs is irrelevant to the cellular inactivation methods.

Encouraged by the successful in vitro differentiation between live and dead cells using OSiNDs, we have further investigated OSiNDs for in vivo live/dead cell differentiation. For this purpose, zebrafish was selected as a model organism. Similar to the in vitro results, only dead zebrafish embryos/adult s emitted strong green fluorescence (Figs. 3d and S9), indicating that the OSiNDs can be used to screen healthy embryos and adults. Moreover, we have also investigated the feasibility of using OSiNDs in the real-time monitoring of drug toxicity/efficacy in vivo. A red fluorescent anticancer drug doxorubicin (Dox), which has well-known systemic toxicity, was chosen for this purpose. As revealed in Fig. S10, the red fluorescent Dox molecules were quickly endocytosed by the zebrafish and distributed over the whole body at 1 h without noticeable toxicity since the zebrafish cells were hardly stained by dead cell-targeting OSiNDs. As the incubation time increased, besides the original whole-body distribution, the Dox molecules gradually accumulated in a small circular area, which may be the yolk, to exert their toxic action, which was proved by the enhanced green fluorescence of OSiNDs in the circular area. Finally, it was found that the Dox presented severe systemic toxicity, as revealed by the whole-body distribution of the OSiNDs. It is believed that such a dynamic visualization technique for evaluating drug toxicity will find applications in the biomedical field.

Furthermore, we have evaluated the cytocompatibility of OSiNDs using S. aureus and A549 cells as representatives of the microbial and mammalian cells, respectively. The parallel tests for PI were also carried out for comparison. As shown in Figs. 3i–k, PI exhibited apparently higher cytotoxicity to S. aureus cells than OSiNDs, while OSiNDs and PI had negligible toxicity to A549 cells within 24 h. The above results indicate the higher safety of OSiNDs than that of PI, ensuring the suitability of OSiNDs for bioimaging.

In summary, we have designed ultrabright green fluorescent OSiNDs for wash-free, fast, and efficient discrimination between live and dead cells both in vitro and in vivo. The fluorescent probe could realize universal staining of dead bacterial, fungal, and mammalian cells. The live/dead discrimination capability of OSiNDs might be attributed to the different internalization pathways of the probe; the OSiNDs were slowly endocytosed by live cells, whereas they rapidly diffused into the dead cells and strongly interacted with intracellular DNA/RNA, thus labeling the DNA/RNA of the dead cells. Compared with the commercial probes, the OSiNDs could be facilely prepared at low cost and exhibited excellent photostability and biocompatibility, making them a suitable candidate for long-term monitoring of cell viability. Combining the green-emitting OSiNDs with red-emitting SYTO 60 (a commercial dye that stains both live and dead cells), we have developed a sensitive and precise quantification method for live and dead cells. Furthermore, the high sensitivity to cell viability and excellent optical properties of OSiNDs endowed the probe with the capability of real-time drug toxicity/efficacy evaluation in vivo, which will provide a new solution for assessing the therapeutic performance of the drugs. Taking advantages of the OSiNDs with the favorable optical properties and modifiable surfaces, new OSiND-based biosensors may be developed by conjugating various functional agents for labeling or detecting biomolecules, cells, or biofilms. In addition, OSiNDs can also be used to construct supramolecular structures for carrying various drugs for treating diverse diseases. We believe that the fluorescent nanodot-based universal cell imaging probe will find wide applications in biology and medicine.
The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21673037) and Scientific Research Foundation of the Graduate School of Southeast University (YBJJ1777).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2019.05.031.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

One-step synthesis of superbright water-soluble silicon nanoparticles with photoluminescence quantum yield exceeding 80%
Adv. Mater. Interfaces
Supramolecular nanogel-based universal drug carriers formed by “soft-hard” co-assembly: accurate cancer diagnosis and hypoxia-activated cancer therapy
Adv. Therap.
Smart supramolecular “Trojan horse”-inspired nanogels for realizing light-triggered nuclear drug influx in drug-resistant cancer cells
Adv. Funct. Mater.
One-step synthesis of ultrasmall and ultrabright organosilica nanodots with 100% photoluminescence quantum yield: long-term lysosome imaging in living, fixed, and permeabilized cells
Nano Lett.
Toxicity and internalization of CuO nanoparticles to prokaryotic alga Microcystis aeruginosa as affected by dissolved organic matter
Environ. Sci. Technol.
Subcellular fate of a fluorescent cholesterol-poly(ethylene glycol) conjugate: an excellent plasma membrane imaging reagent
Langmuir

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xiaokai Chen is a PhD candidate at Southeast University (Nanjing, P. R. China). Her current research interest focuses on ﬂuorescent nanomaterials.
Xiaodong Zhang is a PhD candidate at Southeast University (Nanjing, P. R. China). His current research interest focuses on nanomaterials and nanomedicines.
Chengcheng Li is an associate professor at Jiangnan University (Wuxi, P. R. China). Her current research interest focuses on biomaterials and biotechnology.

Sayed Mir Sayed is a postdoctoral fellow at Southeast University (Nanjing, P. R. China). His current research interest focuses on the synthesis of organic luminescent materials for biomedical and optoelectronic applications.
Wei Sun is a master’s degree candidate at Southeast University (Nanjing, P. R. China). His current research interest focuses on nanomaterials.
Fengming Lin is an associate professor at Southeast University (Nanjing, P. R. China). Her major research interest focuses on bioengineering and biotechnology.
Fu-Gen Wu is a professor at Southeast University (Nanjing, P. R. China). His major research interest focuses on cell imaging and biomaterials.
